| P. 4 Integrated Report of ACS Group 2024

Florentino Pérez Chairman of the ACS Group

2024 has been a decisive year for the ACS Group, which has consolidated its position as a global leader in the infrastructure sector. With a clear strategic focus and integrated organisation, we have achieved excellent results that reinforce our commitment to innovation and transformation in the sectors in which we operate. The result is that, through our projects, ACS has driven the modernisation of key infrastructure, creating jobs and contributing to economic and social progress.

Thanks to the constant efforts of over 157,000 people who comprise our Group, we have achieved an unprecedented volume of sales: EUR 41,633 million, an increase of 16.5% on the previous year. This growth, based on our outstanding international presence, has positioned us among the leading Spanish companies with the greatest global expansion, reflecting the leadership of our companies in the main markets.

This progress has also yielded outstanding operating results, with EBITDA up 28.7% to EUR 2,456 million and net attributable profit of EUR 828 million, 6.1% higher than in 2023. These data are not just figures; they reflect a strategy that combines investment, sustainability and profitability, as set out in our Strategic Plan 2024-2026.

In particular, the total return for our shareholders in 2024 exceeded 26.9%, as a result of dividends paid during the year and a 20.6% annual revaluation in our share price. Over the last five years, the average annual return has been 14.1%, while over the last 25 years we have achieved an average return of 12.7%.

We are confident that this positive trend will continue. The context is favourable because the demand for traditional and new generation infrastructure - linked to sectors such as digitalisation, mobility or new energy sources - continues to grow.

We are very well positioned, as evidenced by the fact that our total project backlog reached EUR 88,209 million, up 19.9% compared to 2023.

This success is also built on strong alliances with the best partners. In 2024, we have strengthened our shareholder base with the incorporation of CriteriaCaixa, an international benchmark, and the subsequent appointment of its chairman, Isidro Fainé, as vice- chairman of our Group.

But beyond economic achievements, we have experienced moments of deep reflection and solidarity. We were deeply shaken by the havoc wreaked by the DANA floods in Valencia, a disaster that devastated the lives of the Valencian people and touch all Spaniards. In the face of adversity, we responded with solidarity and, through our Foundation and various Group companies, we made ourselves available to the public administrations to work in the rebuilding of damaged infrastructure and the reactivation of the affected areas. This is the spirit that defines ACS: a commitment to society that transcends mere business activity.

This year we also suffered the sad loss of Javier Echenique, a director of the company, whose 20 years of dedication to the growth and expansion of the Group have left an indelible mark on us all. His legacy lives on in our culture and values, which guide our every step.

We enter 2025 with the same enthusiasm and commitment that have brought us this far and that will continue to be key to the evolution of our company. We will continue to work to ensure that shareholders, customers, employees, institutions and suppliers continue to trust in the future of our Group.

Growth, integration and future reflect the evolution of the ACS Group in 2024, a year in which we have continued to strengthen the foundations of a cohesive, expanding project committed to profitability.

In the year, we achieved a net profit of EUR 828 million and our sales increased by 16.5% to over EUR 41,600 million. In addition, our project backlog has grown by 20% year-on-year and reached an record high: EUR 88,209 million. Approximately 60% of this figure comes from the United States, where we are established leader in the sector.

By business area, the companies specialising in Integrated Solutions contributed EUR 536 million to the Group's results, while the Engineering and Construction and Infrastructure segments contributed EUR 156 million and EUR 205 million, respectively.

Similarly, in 2024, the Group has generated a net operating cash flow of almost EUR 2,100 million, an increase of over EUR 1,000 million compared with the previous year. This is undoubtedly one of the most important indicators because it measures our ability to invest, grow and remunerate our shareholders.

The data shows that we continue to make progress on the targets set out in our Strategic Plan 2024-2026, presented last April, in which we set ourselves the goal of generating up to EUR 4,000 million net operating cash flows and up to EUR 1,000 million of net profit by 2026. A solid roadmap to boost our growth through three key pillars: ACS's global leadership in high-growth and high added value segments; the reduction of the Group's risk profile; and the boost to investment in infrastructure, both traditional and new generation.

In 2024 we have made great strides in this direction. For example, the awards made to the Group in high-growth segments such as digitalisation, mobility, biopharma and critical metals, among others, already account for 50% of the total. In addition, we have continued to push for contracts with a low risk profile, which represent more than 85% of our current backlog.

| P. 6 Integrated Report of ACS Group 2024

“Investment is the driver for our growth and profitability”

Investment, the driver for our future growth and profitability, has established itself as one of our greatest strengths. In this line, our strategy for the development of data centres stands out, which has made it possible for us to already have a pipeline of 2.1GW of projects under development. In addition, we have identified opportunities for an additional 4GW in key regions in Europe, Australia and the United States.

At the same time, we have taken steps in the natural resources sector, where CIMIC has acquired an additional 10% holding in Thiess, in which it now holds 60% of the capital, giving it effective control of its operations and investment policy.

In the area of traditional infrastructure, we have also made significant progress, such as the award to a consortium led by Iridium, our concession development subsidiary, of a project to finance, build and operate for 50 years more than 25 km of managed lanes on the SR400 highway in Atlanta (Georgia).

Meanwhile, Abertis has continued to grow in the transport concessions operating segment, increasing its sales by 9.8% in 2024 to over EUR 6,000 million. The company plans to generate more than EUR 27,000 million in cash by 2033, which will allow it to maintain its current dividend policy over the next decade and continue to invest in new assets. This commitment to growth is evidenced, for example, by transactions such as the recent acquisition of a majority holding in the French A-63 toll motorway, which we closed in early 2025.

To supplement these strategic levers, we have continued to focus on adding specialised engineering and systems capabilities. This includes the acquisitions of engineering and mining services companies announced by Turner, Sedgman, Thiess and CIMIC.

We have also enhanced our operational efficiency through strategic integration and resource rationalisation. An example of progress in this area is the merger between Flatiron and Dragados North America, which led to the creation of the second largest civil engineering contractor in the United States.

The high cash flow generated by the ACS Group and its solid financial position, with a net debt of less than 0.3 times the Group's consolidated EBITDA, provide us with a capacity of more than EUR 6,000 million until 2030, i.e. around EUR 1,000 million per year without increasing our debt, to undertake future investments that will allow us to capitalise on the opportunities offered by the market.

And we plan to do so while intending to maintain attractive returns for shareholders. In line with this, the Board of Directors has proposed an increase in shareholder remuneration against the 2024 results, which is a clear demonstration of our commitment to value creation and the strength of our business model.

We are aware that all this is only possible thanks to the talent of our professionals, who have become ACS' main competitive edge. That is why this year we have gone a step further by launching ACS University, an educational platform that draws on the experience of our companies and with which we want to offer employees opportunities to develop new skills and continue to grow professionally. Enhancing their specialisation and training is particularly important in the context of the sophistication of the markets in which we operate.

This talent will also be key to ensuring the sustainability of our processes and products. We continue to take steps in this direction, as evidenced by our ambitious environmental targets, which include achieving climate neutrality by 2045. Acknowledgements such as the ACS Group's inclusion in the Dow Jones Sustainability Index (DJSI) in 2024 reinforce our conviction that this is the way forward.

We know that our global presence, our offer of high added value projects and the talent we have place us in a magnificent position to continue contributing value to our different stakeholders. This value involves, among other things, continuing to grow and increase the size of ACS. That is my goal and my challenge as CEO of a Group that has increased its market capitalisation twofold in the last three years, and whose ambition is to continue contributing to building a better future.

SHAREHOLDER RETURN OVER THE LAST 3 YEARS

The ACS Group is a global infrastructure operator and provider comprising leading companies in the development, building and operation of engineering, construction and infrastructure development projects in the transportation, environment, building, digital, natural resources and advanced technology segments.

The ACS Group is formed of over 157,000 employees who work in an integrated manner in the three most highly developed markets in the world (North America, Asia-Pacific and Europe), generating synergies and ensuring that ACS is present along the entire value chain of the infrastructure business.

With sales of more than EUR 41,600 million in 2024 and order backlog of some EUR 88,200 million at year-end, the Group's international track record has once again been recognised by the prestigious Engineering News- Record (ENR) magazine. Specifically, ACS is a leader in the data centre, biopharma, chemical and mining sectors, as well as in the rail infrastructure, motorways, airports, bridges and sports and commercial facilities segment. Also in the concessions segment, as a greenfield assets developer and brownfield infrastructure operator.

With an approach based on adapting to the various markets and each project’s needs, the Group’s companies have a common corporate culture and values geared towards excellence, added value creation, profit generation and innovation, which contributes to the advancement of the communities in which it operates.

The ACS Group's global presence, together with its positioning in new-generation sectors and its vast knowledge of engineering, civil engineering and building, places the Group in an extraordinary position to embrace the opportunities offered by the market and transform the future of infrastructure.

INTEGRATED SOLUTIONS INFRASTRUCTURE ENGINEERING AND CONSTRUCTION

Digitalisation, demographics, deglobalisation and energy demand

The design, construction and modernisation of infrastructure continues to emerge as a pillar of socio-economic development and remains high on the agenda of governments, businesses and investors.

Phenomena such as digitalisation, demographic changes or new energy demand offer opportunities for the development of new infrastructure that connects people, contributes to create new city models that meet citizens' needs and, ultimately, facilitate progress.

Demand for high added value infrastructure also continues to grow due to the de-globalisation or relocation of key industries in the world's main developed markets, mainly in strategic sectors such as pharmaceuticals, healthcare, manufacturing, technology, defence and aviation, among others.

In the field of digitalisation, opportunities lie in areas such as the creation of solutions for digital transformation, the deployment of telecommunications networks and the creation of semiconductor manufacturing facilities. Also key is the development of data centres, essential infrastructure to enable the storage, processing and management of large volumes of information.

Specifically, in the data centre sector, demand is expected to exceed 139 GW globally by 2028, driven by generative artificial intelligence (AI) and the migration of information to the cloud from traditional storage sources. AI will not only drive this segment but will also serve as a tool to leverage the large volumes of information available when making project life-cycle decisions.

The sectors of sustainable mobility, transportation and energy demand will also attract significant investment in the coming years, driven by the need to move towards production models with less impact on the environment and more liveable cities. In these areas, the development of advanced energy storage systems, the creation of battery recycling plants, innovation in new technologies such as hydrogen, the electrification of transportation and the creation and deployment of smart urban mobility systems, among others, are important.

In the building sector, there are already opportunities linked to industrialised construction, which offers advantages such as shorter build times, lower costs, use of more sustainable materials and a reduction in the volume of waste generated.

Automation and industrialised construction

Enabling the deployment of mature technologies, a key challenge for infrastructure

Alongside the high demand and opportunities mentioned above, there are challenges facing the industry. These include the need to anticipate and reduce bottlenecks in infrastructure and the supply chain to avoid slowing down the progress and deployment of mature technologies. Meeting this challenge is key, for example, for the advancement of electric vehicles, which is highly dependent on the development of charging infrastructures and improved energy storage systems.

This challenge is also relevant in activities such as the sourcing of critical minerals for the energy transition, where it will be necessary to continue to increase supply capacity to enable the sustainable reindustrialisation of countries and the relocation of key industries.

In 2024, the ACS Group presented its 2024-2026 Strategic Plan. This roadmap combines sustainable growth of the company — through an ambitious investment plan — with maintaining attractive shareholder remuneration. All of this in line with a business model that has allowed the Group to accelerate its evolution from a construction company to an engineering group capable of meeting both the sophisticated demand for new generation infrastructure and traditional buildings and concessions.

In 2024, the ACS Group continued to take steps along the lines envisaged in its Strategic Plan. Throughout the year, ACS continued to boost its positioning in high- growth and high added value segments such as infrastructure linked to digitalisation, energy, defence, mobility, biopharma and critical metals. Projects linked to these segments represented over 50% of new orders in the year.

ACS has also continued to work to keep the risk profile of its backlog low. These projects accounted for more than 85% of the total by the end of 2024.

The Group has also made progress in investing in the development of traditional and new-generation infrastructure, addressing long-term megatrends such as digitalisation, energy demand and demographic changes. The company already has a pipeline of 2.1 GW of data centre projects under development and is exploring opportunities for an additional 4 GW in key regions, mainly the US.

In the area of traditional infrastructure, we must note the award, together with other partners, of a project to finance, build and then operate, for 50 years, over 25 km of managed lanes on the SR400 highway in Atlanta, Georgia.

Alongside these strategic lines, the company continues to incorporate specialised engineering and systems capabilities to develop complementary activities in the value chain.

Under this premise, in 2024, Turner announced the acquisition of Dornan Engineering, the third largest electromechanical engineering company in Europe, with a EUR 1,100 million pipeline in data centres, biopharma, life and industrial sciences. Sedgman acquired Prudentia Engineering, a company specialising in the chemical and energy industry and mineral processing, and MinSol Engineering, a company with more than 15 years’ experience in developing the global lithium industry.

In addition, Thiess acquired PYBAR, one of Australia's largest underground metals mining contractors, and Mintrex, a leader in engineering and project and asset management services.

Leighton also announced the acquisition of Maverick, an engineering consultancy specialising in digital infrastructure, advanced technology and high-rise buildings.

Moreover, the Strategic Plan 2024-2026 envisages that the ACS Group will continue to promote operating efficiency through strategic integration, rationalisation of resources and simplification. As an example of the progress made on this roadmap, it is worth highlighting the integration between Flatiron and Dragados North America, which has made possible the creation of the second largest civil engineering contractor in the United States.

Under this new strategic approach, the company aims to reach EUR 1,000 million of net profit by 2026 and to generate between EUR 3,300 and 4,000 million of cash, increasing shareholder remuneration. In this way, ACS Group will be able to continue to offer an attractive return to its shareholders while increasing investment in transportation, social and new generation infrastructure projects.

The plan envisages the Group's profit growing at a rate of 8% to 10% per year over the period, to over EUR 48,000 million by 2026.

The ACS Group's business model aims to create shared value for all stakeholders, focusing on shareholders and investors, the people who make up the company, clients, partners and suppliers, administrations and regulatory authorities, and the communities in which ACS companies operate.

Through a robust risk control model, the ACS Group operates in an integrated manner in the three most developed markets in the world: North America, Asia- Pacific and Europe.

In line with the Strategic Plan 2024-2026, which deepens the Group's operational integration and simplification, the organisational structure is aligned with the management model and based on three areas, which provides a clear view of ACS's growth vectors and contributes to an optimal allocation of capital.

The wide range of companies and sectors in which the ACS Group operates enables all its subsidiaries to carry out integrated activities, which allows it to achieve synergies at a global level and to lead large-scale projects. 61% 21%

The ACS Group has evolved from a company specialising in construction to an operator and provider of infrastructure worldwide. The Group comprises leading companies in developing, constructing and operating engineering, civil engineering and building projects and new-generation solutions in sectors of the future such as digitalisation, smart mobility and energy demand.

ACS carries out its operations through companies with a cross-cutting activity that cover different sectors, allowing it to create synergies between them and offer a complete service to its customers.

In line with the Strategic Plan 2024-2026, one of the ACS Group's main pillars is the promotion of new-generation infrastructure projects in all its markets.

Through the companies Turner and CIMIC, the ACS Group designs, builds and operates integral projects with high added value: from technological solutions such as data centres to telecommunications networks, as well as facilities for the production of semiconductors, solutions for the defence, health, biopharma and sports industries, and projects related to energy demand such as renewable assets, battery factories, power transmission lines and energy storage systems.

These segments are in high demand as a result of technological advances, new energy models and demographic changes. In fact, 50% of the ACS Group's new contracts awarded in 2024 were in this business area.

Turner is the leading construction management company in the United States. It has extensive experience in engineering, supply-chain management and logistics, which are key components of large-scale projects.

Specifically, the company specialises in the data centre, healthcare, education, airport, sports and commercial office segments. It also plays a key role in the manufacturing sector by building infrastructure for the electric vehicle industry by manufacturing and recycling batteries, as well as other facilities that supply this market.

In 2024, Turner was named best contractor in the United States for the fourth consecutive year, according to the prestigious US Engineering News-Record (ENR) magazine. It also topped the green contractor rankings as the company that delivered the most projects with a low environmental impact, high energy efficiency or low carbon footprint. In addition, Newsweek magazine selected Turner as one of America's most trusted companies in 2024.

Last year, the company announced its entry into the European market through the acquisition of the Irish firm Dornan Engineering, the third largest electromechanical engineering company in Europe, with a EUR 1,100 million backlog in data centres, biopharma and life and industrial sciences.

CIMIC is a leading engineering, natural resources, services and infrastructure development company primarily in the Asia-Pacific region. The company operates through various firms and offers a wide range of high-tech solutions focused on infrastructure feasibility, design, planning, construction, maintenance, asset management, restoration and dismantling of infrastructure.

CIMIC's strategic acquisitions in recent years have given it access to new opportunities in energy and natural resources. In 2024, CIMIC increased its holding in Thiess by 10% to 60% of the capital of this company specialising in the natural resources sector.

CIMIC operates through the following companies:

CPB Contractors (Asia-Pacific) is one of the largest civil engineering and construction contractors undertaking complex infrastructure projects in Australia and New Zealand.

The ACS Group's governance model is based on ACS's corporate values as established in its Code of Conduct: integrity, excellence, trust. These values, which serve as the basis for the culture of ethics and regulatory compliance that has governed the Group's activities since its inception, inspire the set of rules, policies and procedures that make up the ACS Governance System, which includes the regulation of the composition, structure, competencies and functioning of the Company's governing bodies, as well as the relations between these bodies and their members and executives.

Based on these values, the regulations that make up the ACS Governance System are articulated and are essentially set out in:   The Company By-laws, which establish the fundamental rules and principles guiding the organisation and functioning of the Company’s management bodies, and are implemented through the Shareholders’ General Meeting By-laws and the Rules of the Board of Directors.

The ACS Group's Code of Conduct, the supreme rule, which presides over the organisation's entire body of regulations, with application for the entire the ACS Group.

The corporate policies aimed at establishing the principles and criteria that must serve as a basis for the governance model of the Company and its Group and how they conduct their activities.

The Compliance and Risk policies and procedures that make up the Global Compliance Management System and the Comprehensive Risk Control and Management System, which, respecting the Group's highly decentralised management structure, enable ACS, as the Group's parent company, to define, evaluate and supervise the different areas of Compliance, as well as the financial and non-financial risk control and management systems at Group level, including strategic and geopolitical, operational,

The ACS Group's commitment to ethical and sustainable action

Transparency of information to shareholders, the market and other stakeholders, under the principles of truthfulness, clarity and speed technological, legal, criminal and anti-bribery (Compliance), sustainability, reputational, financial and tax or corruption-related risks. These procedural policies establish and implement ACS' commitment to compliance with the law, regulatory requirements, efficient risk management, industry codes, internal regulations, good governance standards, generally accepted best practices, ethics and the expectations of its stakeholders.

Regulations of the of the General Meeting

The ACS Group's General Shareholders' Meeting, as the supreme body expressing the will of the Company, is the main channel for shareholder engagement in the Company. Its decisions are taken in accordance with applicable laws and the Company By-laws and the Shareholders’ General Meeting By-laws and are binding on all shareholders.

The General Shareholders' Meeting is responsible, among other functions, for approving the annual accounts, the management report, which shall include the mandatory information on sustainability, the distribution of profits, the approval of corporate management and the appointment and removal of directors, as well as any other functions attributed to it by law or the ACS internal regulations. The Company is responsible for ensuring equal treatment of all shareholders who are in the same position with regard to the rights to information, attendance, participation and the exercise of voting rights at the General Meeting.

The ACS Board of Directors represents the Company, conducts its businesses and carries out any transactions that constitute its purpose or are related to it, in addition to its non-delegable functions in accordance with the Law and the By-Laws.

Without prejudice to the foregoing, the policy of the Board of Directors is to focus its activity on the general function of defining the strategic and management guidelines for the Company, taking into account the projection of the same on ACS Group companies, entrusting the administrative and management bodies of the companies forming part of the Group with the functions of day-to-day management and effective management thereof, without prejudice to establishing the necessary mechanisms for the exchange of information and coordination, also ensuring that the corporate interest of the Company is reconciled with that of these entities.

In the exercise of its duties, the Board of Directors acts with unity of purpose and independence of judgement, treating all shareholders in the same position equally, and is guided by corporate interest, understood as the achievement of a profitable and sustainable business in the long term, thus creating value in the Company and its Group, also taking into consideration the other stakeholders that may be affected by its activity.

As of the date of this Report, the ACS Board of Directors is made up of 14 members of recognised prestige and professional competence who act with independent judgement in the performance of their duties, having a diversity of skills, experience, training and gender, among other aspects, thus ensuring overall suitability for the best performance by the Board and its Committees of their respective duties. The main changes in its composition in 2024 were the incorporation of Mr Isidro Fainé as proprietary director appointed at the proposal of the significant shareholder Criteria Caixa, S.A.U., a director who is also chairman of the aforementioned significant shareholder and of the "La Caixa" Foundation; the voluntary resignation of the independent director Mr Antonio Botella; and the death of the proprietary director Mr Javier Echenique, who devoted 20 years to the promotion and expansion of the ACS Group.

In line with the spirit of continuous improvement in corporate governance, the Board of Directors undergoes an annual evaluation of its operations, as well as that of its Committees, in order to identify areas for improvement and develop action plans for their implementation and monitoring.

To make its functions more effective, the Board has the following committees, the members of which are appointed taking into account the knowledge, skills and experience necessary for the optimum performance of their duties and in accordance with applicable regulations.

Executive Committee: Delegated Committee of the

Board of Directors that exercises, by delegation of the Board of Directors, all powers that correspond to the Board except those that are legally or statutorily non- delegable.

Audit and Sustainability Committee: Internal Board

Committee that is attributed, in accordance with the legal provisions and with the recommendations of good governance, functions relating to:

Oversee financial and non-financial reporting, internal control and internal audit and risk management and control.

Maintain relations with the external auditor and the auditor for sustainability information, and submit to the Board proposals for the selection, appointment, re-election and replacement thereof, preserving their independence in the exercise of their functions.

Oversee the Company's Governance System, which includes ACS's policies, rules and internal codes of conduct, among others, in the areas of compliance, corporate governance, sustainability, information security and privacy, data protection, risks and internal management and control systems.

Report on related-party transactions that must be approved by the General Meeting or the Board and supervise the internal reporting and periodic checks procedure established by the Company for those transactions for which approval has been delegated by the Board in accordance with the applicable regulations.

Reporting to the Board on all matters envisaged in the law, the Company By-Laws and the Rules of the Board of Directors.

Appointments Committee: Internal Board Committee that is attributed, in accordance with the legal provisions and with the recommendations of good governance, functions relating to:

The selection and appointment of directors and senior management.

The positions and structure of the Board.

The annual evaluation of the Board and its committees.

Design and structuring of regular refresher programmes for directors.

Remuneration Committee: Internal Board Committee that is attributed, in accordance with the legal provisions and with the recommendations of good governance, functions relating to:

Proposing to the Board the remuneration policy for directors and senior executives and reviewing it periodically.

Proposing the contractual conditions of executive directors and senior executives’ contracts in terms of remuneration.

Reporting to the Board on the setting of the individual remuneration of each director.

Reporting on the proposal of multi-year plans that may be established.

Verifying the information on directors' and senior executives’ remuneration contained in corporate documents.

To ensure that potential conflicts of interest do not adversely affect the independence of external advice provided to the Committee.

In accordance with the Rules of the Board of Directors, the Audit and Sustainability Committee, the Nominations Committee and the Remuneration Committee establish an annual work plan that includes their main activities during the year, and prepare an annual report on their activity during the year, including, among other matters, any significant activities carried out during the period, which is published on the corporate website upon the publication of the call for the Ordinary General Shareholders' Meeting.

On 16 January 2025, ACS, Actividades de Construcción y Servicios, S.A. agreed to carry out the second capital increase charged to reserves approved by the shareholders at the 2024 General Shareholders Meeting. Shareholders could choose between receiving cash (at EUR 0.454/share) or new shares. 41.01% of the free allotment rights were sold to ACS, resulting in a dividend payment of EUR 50,582,972.96 on 6 February 2025. Finally, 1,378,681 new shares were issued with a simultaneous increase and redemption of EUR 689,340.50, with no impact on the total number of shares outstanding.

In addition, on 7 January 2025, Turner Corporation completed the acquisition of Dornan Engineering for EUR 410 million in cash. Dornan, which specialises in engineering for high-tech projects in Europe, will be consolidated in ACS's financial statements in 2025 under IFRS 3. The value of assets, liabilities, goodwill and intangibles is not yet available. The goodwill is attributed to Dornan's experience and growth potential in the European technology sector. With this acquisition, Turner seeks to accelerate its expansion in Europe, offering turnkey solutions with its low-risk construction business model in the high-end technology market.

Lastly, the talks held in 2024 resulted in an agreement announced on 28 February 2025, whereby Abertis will acquire a 51.2% interest in the A-63 highway in France, a strategic corridor between Spain and northern Europe. The transaction strengthens the Group’s backlog and financial soundness and contributes to its growth and cash flow replacement strategy. The transaction will be concluded subject to regulatory approvals.

ACS is a global leader in the development of infrastructures, key sectors for society with the aim of building a better future. Its commitment to the social and economic progress of the countries in which it operates is realised through the development, financing and operation of engineering, civil works and construction projects, as well as new generation solutions in high added value sectors such as energy transition, digitalisation and smart mobility. The promotion of sustainable development is one of the fundamental pillars of its corporate strategy with the aim of generating shared value for all ACS stakeholders.

Thus, in the area of Sustainability, the ACS Group's strategy is based on a series of common objectives developed in the 2025 Sustainability Master Plan approved by the Board of Directors in 2021 and on those defined in the Group's Sustainability Policy. The different companies, under these common principles and with autonomy in their areas of competence, manage their resources as efficiently as possible in order to contribute to the achievement of the objectives.

This 2025 Sustainability Master Plan sets out the Group’s three strategic priorities and the twelve commitments with regard to sustainability that the ACS Group has set itself to continue driving the overall sustainability in infrastructure:

These three commitments include 26 strategic lines and 38 objectives for 2025 with the aim of continuing to create shared value and maintaining the ACS Group’s position as a global leader in the infrastructure sector.

Considering the requirements of stakeholders in terms of sustainability and the material issues identified, the

ACS Group has established 17 of these objectives as priorities for 2025. These objectives are established at Group level based on the business model of each of the companies that make up the Group, as well as ACS's sustainability strategy.

In 2024, the ACS Group carried out an exhaustive Double Materiality Analysis in order to identify and understand in depth its impact on the environment and the external factors that may influence its performance. Although this analysis responds to the requirements of the European Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), ACS has gone beyond mere regulatory compliance, turning it into a strategic opportunity to continue integrating sustainability into the Group's DNA.

This process has been developed with the collaboration of all the Group's companies and the active participation of the heads of the specialised areas, ensuring an integrated approach aligned with the ACS Group's business model. In addition, the opinion of the main stakeholders has been taken into account, allowing us to understand their expectations and needs in order to strengthen the relationship with them and respond to their concerns.

The analysis has been carried out in full alignment with the standards of the European directive, ensuring a rigorous approach in line with international best practice.

A key exercise in sustainability leadership

The main objective of this analysis is to carry out an exhaustive assessment of all ACS Group companies in order to identify the Impacts, Risks and Opportunities and to determine the material issues to be developed in the sustainability report, from a dual perspective:

Financial materiality: analyses how the environment influences the development of the ACS Group, its financial position and performance, cash flows, access to financing and cost of capital.

Impact materiality: analyses how the ACS Group's different activities affect the environment and people in the short, medium or long term, both positively and negatively.

This analysis facilitates the integration of material sustainability issues as a key element of the ACS Group's strategy and decision-making.

The main objective of the Double Materiality Analysis is to obtain an accurate and up-to-date view of the state of sustainability at ACS, identifying the issues that are material to the Group and serving as a basis for consolidating a leading sustainability strategy in the sector.

Beyond being an analysis exercise, Double Materiality becomes a key tool for strategic decision making. It allows us to strengthen those aspects in which ACS is already generating a positive impact and, at the same time, to anticipate and mitigate the risks that may affect its economic and reputational performance, as well as to minimise the negative impact that we may have on the environment.

An integrated approach aligned with the Group's reality

To ensure a truly representative analysis, ACS has comprehensively assessed all sustainability issues proposed by the CSRD. In addition, two specific strategic aspects have been added for the Group: Donations and Foundations and Innovation and Digitalisation.

The ACS Group integrates efficient resource management and environmental protection into its business objectives, operating under the principles of precaution and conservation of the natural environment to minimise the impact of its operations. Group companies carry out various activities, all of which are directly dependent on natural resources and have an associated impact. However, this impact is not only seen as a challenge, but also as an opportunity to generate shared value and move towards a more sustainable model. Likewise, due to the climate emergency, the ACS Group aims to contribute to the transition to a low carbon economy by promoting products and services that have a smaller impact on the environment and improving the efficiency of processes in its activities.

As a result of these commitments, the company has defined an environmental management framework comprising the Group's Environmental Policy, approved by the Board on 14 November 2018 and updated on 17 December 2024, which is articulated by the different management systems implemented in the Group companies.

For this reason, the main environmental measures implemented by the ACS Group companies are governed by the basic principles of action developed in the policy. These guidelines are flexible enough to accommodate the specific procedures and mechanisms of each of the Group companies.

With this vision in mind, the Group works in several key areas that make up its environmental strategy, as set out below:

ACS does not see environmental challenges as isolated issues, but as an interconnected set of challenges and opportunities. Action in one area generates impacts in others, and it is precisely in this synergy that the Group's true capacity for transformation lies. Each measure taken not only protects the environment, but also creates value for people and society as a whole.

As a global company, the ACS Group is aware of the important role it can play in the fight against climate change. Therefore, the ACS Group has established, as part of the objectives of its Sustainability Master Plan, the promotion of energy efficiency and the reduction of emissions in its business activities, with the objective of achieving climate neutrality (Net Zero) in 2045.

To this end, in 2024, the ACS Group developed a first ACS Group Climate Change Mitigation Transition Plan (CCCTMP), which will continue to be updated, completed and strengthened throughout 2025 and beyond.

Levers and measures applicable to the reduction of Scope 1 and 2 emissions:

1. Efficiency: the acquisition of more efficient machinery, the optimisation of processes through digitalisation and innovation, and the use of sustainable materials that promote the circular economy.

2. Transition to green fuels: ACS is driving the transition to renewable fuels through the acquisition of more efficient equipment and the use of hybrid, electric or hydrogen-based technologies.

3. Green energy: ACS is promoting self-generation and the consumption of renewable energies, as well as the purchase of electricity with guarantee of origin certificates, with a clear focus on decarbonisation.

Levers and measures applicable to the reduction of emissions from scopes 3:

1 Improvements in design and planning: Promote the sustainable design of projects with a focus on embedded and operational carbon reduction, as well as the incorporation of circular economy measures from the planning stage.

2 Efficiency in transportation: ACS promotes local procurement and the use of renewable fuels in logistics and mobility.

3 Low-carbon shopping: ACS is working on a detailed analysis of its supply chain and collaboration with strategic partners committed to sustainability.

4 Sustainable investment: ACS is committed to investing in projects aligned with its commitment to the environment and social welfare.

Transverse lever: Training and awareness-raising

ACS promotes awareness-raising initiatives for all its stakeholders, from employees to suppliers and clients, in order to create a culture of sustainability and ensure that everyone is part of the change.

Beyond greenhouse gas reduction, ACS actively works to minimise other forms of pollution that affect both the environment and public health.

The transition to renewable energy not only reduces the carbon footprint, but also reduces the emission of air pollutants. In addition, ACS implements prevention and remediation measures in all its projects, ensuring the application of best practices to avoid soil and water contamination and mitigate any impact on the communities in which it operates.

Water is a limited resource and essential for environmental balance and human well-being. ACS is aware of the importance of managing this resource responsibly, ensuring its availability for future generations.

The Group works to optimise water use in all its activities by analysing and implementing measures to reduce consumption, promoting the reuse of wastewater and protecting water bodies in the areas where it operates. In this way, ACS minimises its impact on aquatic ecosystems and reinforces its commitment to sustainable water management.

Within the framework of ACS's Environmental Policy, the Group aims to achieve a balance between development and conservation of biodiversity and natural capital, as well as a commitment to prevent deforestation, in the terms described in the environmental policy, focusing especially on the protection of sites of ecological, scenic, scientific or cultural interest.

To this end, the ACS Group analyses the direct operations that affect its relationship with ecosystems and biodiversity, extending the analysis throughout the value chain, including direct and indirect suppliers in the event that the subcontracted activity is critical for biodiversity. Mitigating the impact on ecosystems through prevention, reduction, restoration and offsetting, especially in protected areas or areas of high biodiversity value, is one of the ACS Group's main objectives with nature.

As part of its commitment to sustainability, the ACS Group integrates the efficient use of resources and the circular economy as key elements in its management model. Under the standards established by the ESRS, ACS prioritises the optimisation of the resources used in its activities, minimising the associated environmental impacts and promoting practices that encourage the durability, recyclability and reuse of materials.

The circular economy is positioned as an essential pillar within the ACS Group's strategy to guarantee the long-term sustainability of the infrastructures and services offered by the ACS Group, contributing to the reduction of waste, the preservation of natural resources and the generation of added value throughout the value chain. This approach allows us not only to meet stakeholder expectations, but also to strengthen the ACS Group's resilience and competitiveness in the face of current environmental and regulatory challenges.

This vision is not limited to the Group's direct operations, but extends to its entire value chain. Collaboration with suppliers to promote the use of sustainable materials, the implementation of reuse and recycling strategies in projects and the adoption of innovative solutions in construction are examples of how ACS promotes circularity in the sector.

The Group encourages the purchase of local goods and services, contributing to the development of the local economy. It also promotes the hiring of local talent, providing job opportunities that improve the quality of life of people in the areas where it develops its projects. This is complemented by constant dialogue with communities, ensuring that their needs and expectations are heard and addressed to mitigate any negative impacts of operations.

Governance, innovation and social contribution are key pillars of the ACS Group's sustainability strategy. A robust and transparent management framework ensures that all the Group's actions generate a positive and sustainable impact over time. Sustainability is not just a commitment, but a vision integrated into ACS's corporate structure and culture.

ACS fosters a corporate culture based on respect, integrity and good practices. To this end, it has developed a solid body of internal regulations that establishes the principles of action for all levels of the organisation.

R&D is one of ACS's strategic levers. The integration of digital tools and the development of innovative solutions allow optimising projects, improving operational efficiency and reducing costs, while reinforcing the positive impact in terms of sustainability.

| 5.1. Efficient Management of Risks and Opportunities

The ACS Group considers it essential to have an efficient risk management policy that guarantees the continuity and success of its operations, adequately protects its investments, ensures compliance with its sustainability commitments, and includes policies that support the correct execution of regulations and safeguard the integrity of its information systems. This ensures the Group's continued leadership in the industry and the achievement of its objectives, in line with the Strategic Plan presented in 2024.

In the ACS Group, risk and opportunity management is an integral process based on the Integrated Control and Management System, defined in the Risk Control and Management Policy. The management structure is organised into specific Areas of Responsibility, which are coordinated through the Governance and Compliance Committee. This committee ensures the representation of all areas involved, guaranteeing an aligned and coherent management.

Following the latest update of the Risk Map, the Group has identified geopolitical risks, risks related to its investments, operational risks (bid and contract management), Compliance, cybersecurity and talent attraction and retention risks as the most relevant risks. For all of them, the Group has defined policies and strategies to ensure their adequate mitigation and control.

In the area of sustainability, the Group has conducted a double materiality assessment in accordance with the new Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) criteria and EFRAG's Double Materiality implementation guide. This involves assessing how the environment affects the company economically and reputationally (financial materiality) and how its activities affect the environment positively or negatively (impact materiality). This dual approach allows the ACS Group to adopt a detailed understanding of the Group's status in terms of sustainability, as well as a deeper and more comprehensive look at its responsibility and role in the economic, social and environmental environment.

The Group also understands the importance of managing emerging risks. These include geopolitical risks such as supply chain disruption and shortages of building materials, as well as water scarcity and biodiversity loss. In addition, the group has equipped itself with tools to assess climate change risk, the management of which is critical to the sustainability of its operations. Managing these emerging risks is a long-term strategy that will enable continuous adaptation to a constantly changing environment.

A crucial aspect of the Group's management is the identification of opportunities. The Group's comprehensive perspective strategically positions it to capitalise on them, strengthening its competitiveness and ensuring long-term sustainability. On the geopolitical front, the Group is prepared to respond to defence and reconstruction needs in various regions. It is also committed to energy generation, the development of advanced technological infrastructure and improving the resilience of the built environment. In addition, major opportunities are identified in the mining of lithium, critical minerals and rare earths, which are essential for the above technologies.

| 5.2. General Risk Control and Management Policy

The ACS Group faces multiple intrinsic risks due to the different countries, activities and markets in which it operates and the tasks it performs, which could potentially impair or even prevent it from achieving its objectives and carrying out its strategies successfully. The ACS Group considers Risk Control and Management to be one of the essential pillars of its long-term value creation strategy. Therefore, one of the Group's strategic priorities is to improve and strengthen this management to increase its effectiveness.

The Group's General Risk Control and Management Policy contains the definition of the Comprehensive Risk Control and Management System. This Policy ratifies ACS's commitment, covering the companies in the group of which ACS is the parent, to the development of the necessary capacities so that risks of all kinds are adequately identified, measured, prioritised, managed and controlled. In addition to the regulatory framework established by the Policy, the different Group companies may develop their own within the framework of their respective functional spheres and areas of responsibility, in accordance with their characteristics and the regulatory framework applicable in each case, respecting in all cases the principles and framework regulations of ACS's General Risk Control and Management Policy.

In the update of the Policy approved in December 2024, the role of an ACS Group Chief Risk Officer has been included, who reports, in line with the Organisation's corporate governance model, to both the CEO and the

Audit Committee. Among other duties, the Chief Risk Officer is responsible for promoting the dissemination of the ACS Group's risk management culture within all of its subsidiaries and for overseeing its consistent, structured application, in accordance with the Group's Risk Control and Management Policy. All of this is done in coordination with the corresponding areas of responsibility (Sustainability, Compliance, Finance, Tax, Cybersecurity and Data Protection). Under the basic principles of the Risk Control and Management Policy, the Chief Risk Officer’s role is functionally and hierarchically independent from the lines of business.

Dual System for risk monitoring and control

The ACS Group is structured in accordance with a decentralised management model and carries out its activity through a large group of companies, which share ACS's culture and values, as well as working synergies between them, although each operates independently in its respective functional spheres and areas of responsibility.

In this respect, the heads of each division and Group companies, through the corresponding risk management departments, are assigned the development of the appropriate risk control and management systems and the necessary internal regulations to ensure their implementation and operation, consistent with the Group's guidelines.

Once the risk categories have been defined, it is essential to establish the areas of responsibility for risk management, ensuring that each group within the organisation is clear about their respective risk management obligations. These areas of responsibility are defined in the relevant Group policies, which in turn define the applicable control and management methodology in accordance with the common criteria set out in the Comprehensive Risk Control and Management System.

The ACS Group's risk methodology envisages the identification of the significant risks to which the Group companies may be exposed in each of the areas of responsibility. Risk identification is a systematic process by which potential events or scenarios that may have an adverse impact on the achievement of the organisation's objectives are recognised and defined. This process is a critical step in risk management, as it makes it possible to anticipate and prepare proactive responses to various threats that may arise.

During risk identification, information is gathered from a variety of sources, both internal and external, and a variety of techniques are used to ensure a comprehensive view of potential risks. These techniques vary based on the intrinsic characteristics of the different types of risks.

Once the risks have been identified, their likelihood of occurrence and potential impact, both financial and reputational, are assessed on a uniform scale for all risks. The assessment enables the organisation to develop appropriate strategies to manage risks effectively.

As with identification, the different areas of responsibility will use the appropriate tools to determine the likelihood of occurrence and impact based on the intrinsic characteristics of the risks assessed by each of them. This is done by collecting data from external and internal sources, using historical data, statistics and expert judgements.

Qualitative scales (e.g. very unlikely, unlikely, probable, very likely) or more precise quantitative analyses may be used. In the impact assessment, financial and reputational aspects, among others, are taken into consideration. As in the likelihood analysis, the impact can be assessed qualitatively (e.g. very low, low, medium, high and very high) or quantitatively through more precise analyses depending on the risk type.

Level of risk that the Group considers acceptable

The ACS Board is aware of the importance of adequate control and management of the risks that affect the achievement of its strategic and operational objectives, and for this reason the ACS Group's General Risk Control and Management Policy aims to achieve a moderate risk profile through prudent management. ACS's business model corresponds to that of a decentralised group diversified by geographic areas, types of assets, portfolios and customers, with a high international presence, mainly in developed countries, maintaining a medium/low risk profile in each of them, and seeking sustainable long-term growth, protecting the organisation's assets and reputation. This model must take into account the expectations of stakeholders.

ACS's different areas of responsibility will be responsible for proposing specific quantitative and qualitative limits for the different risk categories, which will be approved by the Board under the supervision of the Risk Department. They also ensure that these limits are documented and clearly communicated throughout the organisation.

The level of risk that the Group considers acceptable will always be calculated in relation to the level of residual risk, i.e. in relation to the risk that remains considering the implementation of actions and controls for the mitigation of the initial level of risk or the risk inherent in the performance of an activity.

For example, at the proposal of the Risk Department, the ACS Group has determined that certain Design and Construction turnkey contracts are initially incompatible with its risk appetite. Therefore, the areas responsible for business development and risk management in tenders have been informed of the need to consult with the Risk Department before deciding to participate in these types of projects.

Risk control and mitigation mechanisms are the strategies and actions that are implemented to reduce the likelihood of risks occurring and/or to minimise their impact if they materialise, in cases where acceptance of the risk is not possible. The controls can be of varying nature: preventive, detective or corrective, again varying based on the specific risk management area concerned. In addition to controls, impact or probability mitigation strategies and actions include transferring the risk to a third party, e.g. subcontractors or insurance companies, or simply not accepting the risk.

ACS's different areas of responsibility will be responsible for defining and proposing the controls and strategies aimed at maintaining risk levels within the limits established by the Board under the supervision of the Risk Department. As part of this process, existing controls must be analysed. This includes verifying the effectiveness of these controls and determining whether they are adequate or additional measures are required.

The Risk Map of ACS and the Group companies is the document within the Comprehensive Risk Control and Management System that provides a comprehensive view of the different risks to which the Group is exposed. The objective of the Map is to inform all members of the organisation about the main risks affecting the Group and to serve as a guide for monitoring the events associated with these risks, as well as the control and mitigation measures that are in place or will be implemented in the future.

For the preparation of the Risk Map, the different areas of responsibility of the Group's companies share with ACS Risk Department their vision and assessment of the risks they identify within the scope of their responsibility. Based on this information, the Map identifies the main classes of risk that could have an impact on the Group's activities and assesses their potential impact and likelihood.

The ACS Group's Risk Map analyses risks by grouping them into classes. Risk classes are sets of risks that share similar characteristics or nature and are grouped together to facilitate their management and analysis ("Risk Classes"). Each class can include multiple specific risks, allowing risks to be grouped in a more structured and manageable way. Examples of Risk Classes are risks related to information security or risks related to the preparation of tenders.

Accordingly, the Map conveys a comprehensive view of the main classes of risk to which the Group's activities are subject and serves as a guide to the more detailed information provided by the different areas of responsibility.

The Map outlines, for classes of risk whose residual impact could be high or very high, the different controls and mitigation measures that are applied both at Group level and by the different companies comprising the Group. The Risk Department is responsible for the preparation of the Risk Map and its updates, which are carried out at least once a year.

The methodology used for the preparation and updating of the Risk Map includes:

Risk Management, together with corporate officers from different areas, reviews the Risk Classes based on internal and external data, deciding which ones to keep, delete or add.

The corporate managers assess the likelihood and impact of the Risk Classes based on the criteria of the Integrated Risk Management System, including their potential reputational impact. The assessment refers to the residual net risk, taking into account all control and mitigation measures in place.

The Risk Department standardises the results and draws up the Risk Map.

For Risk Classes with high or very high impact, a Risk

Sheet is created with current and additional control and mitigation measures to be implemented.

The ACS Group implements mitigation and control strategies and measures aimed at reducing both the likelihood of occurrence of risks and their possible impact. The areas of responsibility are responsible for defining specific policies, overseeing their implementation and establishing acceptable levels of risk where necessary. These areas work continuously on the analysis and improvement of control and prevention mechanisms, ensuring the resilience and continuity of the Group's operations in the face of possible contingencies. Thanks to these measures, the ACS Group is better prepared to face its risks and protect its interests, as well as those of its stakeholders.

The assessment of risks in the ACS Group's risk map is carried out as follows: the likelihood of a given risk materialising is assessed on a scale of 4 levels of residual likelihood (very unlikely, unlikely, probable, very likely), once the mitigation measures have been considered. For its part, the residual impact is assessed on a scale of 5 levels (very low, low, medium, high, very high), which are defined based on the impact on the net benefit. The potential reputational risk associated with each risk is also assessed. This way of assessing risk exposure is in line with that used to identify and assess the risks associated with environmental, social and governance (ESG) factors, as part of the double materiality assessment and which is included in the Sustainability Report.

For example, in the case of the risk associated with tendering processes, this has been considered an unlikely risk in view of all the policies and measures in place at company and Group level, which ensure a thorough review of all risks associated with the project and the definition of all the measures necessary for their mitigation and control. From the viewpoint of the potential residual impact it is considered high since, although such policies mitigate the potential residual impact to some extent, their main effect is to reduce the likelihood.

Another example is the environmental and circular economy risk, the residual likelihood of which is considered unlikely because the Group, through its sustainability department and other areas of responsibility, ensures that projects implement all necessary mitigation measures to avoid environmental risks. However, in the unlikely event that this risk materialises, the residual impact could potentially be high.

Examples of the assessment of other risks can be found in the sustainability report with reference to climate change risks or the entire double materiality assessment, in both cases based on the methodologies set out there.

In the recent annual update of the Risk Map, and with regard to strategic risks, special attention has been paid to geopolitical risk and risk related to investment/ divestment strategies.

The ACS Group operates in developed countries with stable political and economic systems. In less developed areas, it applies specific policies and control measures. The Management Committee keeps abreast of the global macroeconomic and geopolitical situation, communicating continuously with executives in different geographies to anticipate and respond appropriately to any circumstances. | 5.6. Integrating sustainability into risk management

The ACS Group is aware that the integration of sustainability into risk management has become critically important, largely driven by the new European sustainability and due diligence regulations. The Sustainability Report presents the corresponding analysis in detail and describes the adaptation of our company to the requirements of the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD).

The ACS Group has adopted a comprehensive approach to identifying and assessing risks associated with environmental, social and governance (ESG) factors. To this end, as mentioned above, a double materiality assessment has been conducted in line with the requirements established by the CSRD and EFRAG, in which all material risks related to sustainability are identified.

In addition, the CSRD has established new reporting standards to ensure transparency on sustainability issues. Adaptation to these regulations has involved the implementation of robust frameworks for ESG data collection and reporting, as well as the integration of these aspects into our risk assessment.

The ACS Group considers that the integration of sustainability in its risk management and the adaptation to the new European CSRD regulations are fundamental steps towards the creation of long-term value. These initiatives strengthen its aim to protect not only the interests of the company, but also those of its stakeholders, promoting a fair and sustainable economic environment.

| 5.7. Risks and opportunities arising from climate change

Within this commitment to integrate sustainability into risk management, the risks and opportunities of climate change should be highlighted. To determine and assess climate-related impacts, risks and opportunities, the ACS Group has its own methodology based on the most advanced international standards, mainly the IPCC and TFCD recommendations and ISO14090 and ISO14091. The methodology developed for physical risk analysis allows for risk analysis at project or asset level. During 2023 this methodology was applied to more than 80 significant projects and in 2024 to more than 60 new significant projects across the Group with budgets above EUR 200 M, with a key focus on risk in own operations and verification of the criteria for alignment with the European Taxonomy.

The analysis of transition risks has been performed for own activities and also considering the upstream and downstream value chain. To this end, risks have been assessed at activity and geographical level. This is primarily due to the systemic and sectoral characteristics of these risks as opposed to the physical risks that are declared locally. The application of these methodologies in the years 2022 and 2023 has been used when performing the double materiality assessment in 2024 and for the physical and transitional risk assessment for the new projects in 2024.

The selection of scenarios and time horizons for the risk analysis was made by harmonising the time horizons of the ACS Group's activities and planning with those of the decarbonisation scenarios and targets. The analysis is therefore considered to cover the plausible risks and uncertainties relevant to the Group and to be consistent with the climate-related baseline assumptions used to inform the Group's growth scenarios. For physical risks, climate

projections of the SSP2-RCP4.5 and SSP5-RCP8.5 scenarios have been considered for historical (1986-2018), short-term (< 5 years), medium-term (< 15 years) and long-term (up to 2050) time horizons. SSP2- RCP4.5 has been chosen as representative of current trends and SSP5-RCP8.5 as the worst-case scenario. To assess transition risks, the International Energy Agency's Stated Policies Scenarios (STEPS) and Net Zero Emissions for 2050 (NZE), and the time horizons (2022-2035) and (2035-2050) are used. Similar reasoning applies to this selection as for physical risks.

It has been concluded that, in terms of physical risk, the Group has no material net risk in the short, medium or long term. This is because the gross material risks due to possible extreme weather events in some of the geographical areas in which the Group operates are mitigated by specific measures implemented in all the projects that require them. These measures notably include: transferring risk to customers, specific insurance, contractual clauses covering climate risk, specific health and safety measures, using early warning systems and physical measures against climate impacts, among others, many of which are specified in the contract. In addition, the analysis has led to the conclusion that the company is equipped with the necessary adaptive capacity to cope with the possible evolution of hazards related to long-term physical risks, even for high emission scenarios.

In transition risk, the results indicate some risks associated with policy and law, market or reputation that acquire different levels of intensity depending on time horizons and scenarios. However, the ACS Group currently has a variety of measures (decarbonisation plans with a wide range of levers and measures, close monitoring of regulation, capacity for fast adaptation to the new regulation or anticipation of changes in the market) which enable it to face the possible risks of transition with guarantees in all the scenarios and time horizons analysed.

In addition, the ACS Group has identified major opportunities associated with the transition. Many of them are already an important part of the business and others are being developed as new markets. The ACS Group's vision for 2030 of being the world leader in the critical infrastructure of the present and the future is based on business lines associated with technology for digital transformation, energy transition, sustainable mobility and critical minerals; in other words, sectors closely linked to climate change and with growing demand that are expected to double the value of the group by 2030.

The integration of climate change risks and opportunities into the Comprehensive Risk Control and Management System and the Group's governance has been used to conduct a Group climate change resilience analysis, feeding into the Group's policies, strategies, actions and objectives.

The most significant emerging risks identified by the ACS Group are detailed below. They have all currently materialised, but it is considered that their impact on the development of the Group's activities will be significantly extended over the long term (> 5 years)

Deglobalisation/ geopolitical conflicts: Disruption of the supply chain and shortage of construction materials.

Conflicts in key regions continue to affect global supply chains. For example, tensions in Eastern Europe, the Middle East and Asia have caused significant disruptions. Meanwhile, the long-lasting effects of the COVID-19 pandemic and tensions between major economic powers have exacerbated the energy crisis and increased commodity prices. There is a growing trend towards deglobalisation, with countries and economic blocs prioritising domestic production and supply to reduce their vulnerability to global disruptions.

The construction sector is very exposed to the difficulty of sourcing construction materials. This, together with the increase in raw material costs, the price of energy, the effects of climate change and the increase in preferences and demands of stakeholders, poses an increasingly significant risk in the market. Inadequate use or waste of the natural resources necessary to carry out activities that do not contribute to a circular economy model can lead to scarcity and depletion of resources. Lastly, high demand and a future requirement for the use of specific materials with sustainable characteristics is expected, and it is therefore expected that the Group will again face supply risks from this type of materials over the coming years.

The disruption of the supply chain and the scarcity of construction materials directly affect the development of ACS activities around the world, which may involve delays in the development of projects, breaches of customer demands, failure to comply with the ACS Environmental Policy, as well as reputational risks, non-compliance with regulations and increased production costs.

The incorporation of circular concepts into the production model makes it possible to reduce the intensive use of natural resources and the high pressure on the environment., as well as ACS dependencies.

Resource optimisation also increases operational and financial efficiency, in addition to reducing the waste generated.

In addition, the Environmental Policy and the Group's Sustainability Master Plan define the commitments to encourage the use of recycled construction materials, their durability and efficient waste management.

The objectives of the Sustainability Master Plan included:

Promoting life cycle analysis in infrastructure and building projects, exceeding 200 analysed projects by 2025.

Maintaining a waste recycling rate in excess of 80%.

ACS Group companies participate in various R&D projects related to durability and efficiency in the use of construction resources and materials.

Water is a key resource for both building and social wellbeing. Overexploitation and mismanagement of water resources could lead to water stress and water scarcity.

The water shortage trends observed in Europe, North America and Asia, where the ACS Group operates, could lead to a decrease in water availability, both in terms of quantity and quality.

Delays in construction projects due to limited water availability

Decreased demand for construction projects due to rising water and operational costs   Reputational damage to the construction sector

The main mitigation measures are described in the ACS Group's Environmental Policy and Sustainability Master Plan and include, among others:

Considering and assessing the main risks and impacts in relation to using water resources.

Identifying business opportunities to contribute to the efficient use of water resources, reduce water stress and reduce effluent emissions.

Promoting water consumption reduction initiatives and water recycling/reuse procedures.

Controlling the water extracted from water-stressed areas to minimise its share

Establishing a method for calculating the water footprint

Biodiversity loss and ecosystem degradation

Biodiversity brings value to the economy and society by providing natural resources and capital and ecosystem services. Biodiversity decline and ecosystem degradation can lead to physical, transitional, legal, regulatory and systemic risks for companies.

The construction sector may be exposed to biodiversity transition risks, as its business activities affect the ecosystem and are therefore a potential target for future regulation.

New policies and regulations to protect biodiversity require increased enforcement efforts.

Changes in consumer preferences and increased attention from regulators could negatively affect the construction sector's order book.

Reputational risks in construction activities

The main mitigation measures are described in the ACS Group's Environmental Policy and Sustainability Master Plan.

In collaboration with customers, the public administration and other external stakeholders, the ACS Group promotes implementing measures to identify and minimise the impact on biodiversity. These measures may include physical protection, transplantation or relocation, respect for the life cycles of affected plant and animal species, as well as offsetting the impacts of activities on biodiversity and forest cover through restoration, recovery and reforestation actions.

Chapter 4 of the Statement of Non-Financial Information and Sustainability Information outlines the progress of the ACS Group during 2024 in terms of resilience analysis in relation to biodiversity and the pilot project that has focused on the direct activities and part of the value chain of the construction sector of one of the Group's companies, Dragados.

Shutdown of operating systems due to intrusion into the systems by a third party.   Payment of ransoms for significant amounts or administrative sanctions from public bodies (such as the AEPD). Inability or difficulty in identifying or detecting possible system vulnerabilities that may give rise to cybersecurity threats or attacks.

Having a system with insufficient resources to prevent and mitigate the IT systems’ vulnerabilities.

Absence of a formalised response plan that makes it possible to act efficiently in the event of any attack or threat.
